title: Added '-' syntax to remove volume config option
body:
When using the --volume flag, putting a '-' after the volume removes the volume from the config. 
`--volume /testvol-` removes /testvol from the config. 
Only implemented on Volume flag as a test. 

#1399

comments.body:
Needs documentation changes. 
Needs tests.
@ashley-cui Looks like the tests are failing, because it does not want you to use "spec" as a variable name.  Please fix this and then I will give you a LGTM

Another validation error.
cmd/buildah/config.go:222::warning: declaration of "volVal" shadows declaration at config.go:220 (vetshadow)

@vrothberg The problem with the --remove flag is it is not easy to tie it to any particular volume, and not easy to handle multiple volumes at the same time.

I believe the "-" syntax was decided on based on what Kubernetes was using, as discussed in the issue https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues/1399

I understand it's more complex to implement that via flags but the current approach *can* break existing deployments.

The problem is that `-` is a perfectly fine path. The only invalid character I know of is `/` but that's unfortunately a common user-input when pointing to directories. I would prefer adding dedicated options to avoid such kind of "magic". In this case a `--rm-volume=volume`?

But if others are okay to merge it, I don't want to block (and don't want to be responsible for merging :-)

Well this is a limited case where we would only hit the issue if a volume ended in a '-'.
How about if we allow escaping the field.  
--volume /var/file\- 

Then check if the final character is - and previous char is '\', Of course this would then prevent me from mounting a volume that looked like '/var/file\-'.  

Another thing we could do is check if /var/file- exists, if it does then we mount it, if it does not we look for /var/file and remove it.  That would seem to solve this case best without making the CLI overly complicated.

>  Another thing we could do is check if /var/file- exists, if it does then we mount it, if it does not we look for /var/file and remove it. 

That sounds better. 

> That would seem to solve this case best without making the CLI overly complicated.

I think it depends where we draw the line. Having special cases complicates documentation (sometimes usability) and both can impact the CLI.

I think the other issue is that we want to be able to delete other fields within the image,  

--env foobar- or --label mylabel- 
...
We would end up having to add lots of --rmenv and --rmlabel options.  

Perhaps we can hold off on this until next week and then discuss it in a BlueJeans.
Sounds good to me :+1:
I'll be somewhere in or on a lake then, I'll trust @vrothberg to hold up my side of the argument!

We also need this done for the following options.

--volume
--env
--label
--annotion
--device
--add-host
--dns
--dns-search
--expose
--group-add

Ok after talking to @nalind we think the best way to handle the volume mount of /tmp/foo- is to check if the object exists on disk, if it does, then create a volume, if it does not, then check to see if /tmp/foo exists in the list of volumes and delete it if it does.  If it does not then return an error.

For the future options, then we just check if the option exists and remove it.

Corner cases we will not be able to handle, would be:

1 Adding an option ending with a "-" for anything other then volumes, will not be allowed.  IE You will not be able to add an environment variable with --env foo-, I guss if you add --env foo-="" it would be possible.

2 Remove /tmp/foo volume if /tmp/foo- exists.  Not much we can do about this, and enough of a corner case that I don't think we need to fix.
@rhatdan PTAL. Not sure how to write tests for this since it relies on checking if a file exists though.

podman run -v /tmp/foo- fedora ls /tmp/foo
Should fail. With /tmp/foo is not defined in container

mkdir /tmp/foo-
podman run -v /tmp/foo- fedora ls /tmp/foo-
Should succeed
@ashley-cui Any movement on this one?
@rhatdan Should be implemented, but not sure why the last test is failing, or what it means. I'm re-running it now and seeing what happens.
@rhatdan looks like the test just needed to be re-run. :) This should be all set.

LGTM
@vrothberg are you good with the changes that you requested?

@rh-atomic-bot r+
Good work @ashley-cui 
Now lets get the other options to handle the "-"
Also need to get this functionality into Podman.

Thanks for the ping. I am not a big fan of the new semantics but the changes LGTM. Great work, @ashley-cui.

this seems to have broken things for me:
```sh
$ ctr=$(buildah from ubuntu)
$ buildah run $ctr mkdir /test
$ buildah config --volume /test $ctr
ERRO[0000] Volume cannot be added because it does not exist on disk.
```
@ashley-cui PTAL
@computator does the directory `/test` exist on your host?
No, but volumes are directories in containers, not on the host, so should not be affected by the host.
Hmm okay I may have misunderstood what a volume is.

Okay, under my understanding, a volume usually is a bind mount into a container, which means that the directory does also have to exist on a container? Please correct me if I am wrong. @computator @rhatdan

Okay, under my understanding, a volume usually is a bind mount into a container, which means that the directory does also have to exist on a container? Please correct me if I am wrong. @computator @rhatdan
@ashley-cui Lets talk today, I am in Boston Office.
